title: How a 26-Year-Old Single Mom Is Crushing It in Tech: Hannah White's Story
guestBio: Hannah White is a 26-year-old IT professional at Tomahawk and single mother of two boys. She's navigating the tech industry while balancing the demands of motherhood, demonstrating resilience and determination in building her career. Hannah is also Aimee Rickabus's stepdaughter, bringing a personal perspective to this candid conversation about modern motherhood and professional ambition.
excerpt: At just 26, Hannah White is proving that single motherhood and tech success aren't mutually exclusive. In this raw, honest conversation, she shares how she's building her IT career while raising two boys, the tools that keep her sane, and why owning your power starts with accepting where you are right now.
metaDescription: Single mom Hannah White shares how she's thriving in tech at 26, balancing motherhood and career with practical strategies for modern working mothers.
keyTakeaways: Success in your twenties doesn't have to look like traditional career paths - you can build something meaningful while navigating life's unexpected turns.
Single motherhood can actually strengthen your professional skills by forcing you to become more efficient, organized, and decisive.
Technology and the right systems can be game-changers for managing both work responsibilities and family life effectively.
Owning your power starts with accepting your current circumstances rather than waiting for perfect conditions to pursue your goals.
Building a career in tech is possible at any life stage, even when balancing significant personal responsibilities.
Setting boundaries between work and family time is crucial for maintaining both professional growth and quality parenting.
Finding your identity as both a professional and a mother requires intentional choices about how you spend your time and energy.

pullQuotes.text: You don't have to wait for the perfect moment to own your power - you can start exactly where you are right now.

timestamps.label: Finding your power at any age

content:
What does it mean to own your power in your twenties when life doesn't go according to plan? This week on The Manage Her, I sat down with my stepdaughter Hannah White for one of the most honest conversations we've ever had about modern motherhood, career ambition, and finding your footing when the traditional playbook goes out the window.

At 26, Hannah is navigating waters that many women twice her age find challenging. She's building her career in IT at Tomahawk while raising two young boys as a single mother. But here's what struck me most about our conversation: Hannah isn't just surviving this season - she's actually thriving in ways that might surprise you.

When Life Rewrites Your Script

Hannah's story isn't the linear career trajectory you might expect. Like so many women, she's had to pivot, adapt, and create her own version of success. What I love about her approach is that she's not apologizing for where she is or waiting for some mythical perfect moment to start pursuing her goals.

Too often, we get caught in the trap of thinking we need to have it all figured out before we can step into our power. Hannah is living proof that you can build something meaningful exactly where you are right now. Her journey into tech wasn't planned from age five, but she's embracing it with the kind of intentionality that creates real results.

The reality is that most of our careers won't look like what we imagined in college, and that's not a failure - it's life. Hannah's willingness to lean into the unexpected has opened doors she might never have walked through otherwise.

The Hidden Superpowers of Single Motherhood

Here's something we don't talk about enough: single motherhood can actually make you a better professional. I know that might sound counterintuitive when you're drowning in the daily demands, but Hannah's experience illustrates this beautifully.

When you're the sole decision-maker for your family, you develop skills that translate directly to the workplace. You become incredibly efficient with your time because you have to be. You learn to make decisions quickly and trust your judgment. You develop a level of organization and prioritization that your colleagues notice.

Hannah has had to become the CEO of her own life in a way that many people never experience. Every choice about her career directly impacts her children's future, which gives her a clarity of purpose that's honestly inspiring to watch.

This doesn't mean single motherhood is easy or that we should romanticize the struggle. But it does mean that if you're in this season, you're developing leadership muscles you might not even recognize yet.

Technology as Your Life Management Partner

One of the most practical parts of our conversation centered on the tools and systems that keep Hannah's life running smoothly. As someone working in tech, she has a unique perspective on how to leverage technology not just for work, but for life management.

The key insight here is that you don't need to be a tech expert to use technology strategically. Hannah has identified specific pain points in her daily routine and found apps, systems, and processes that address them directly. She's not using technology for the sake of being high-tech - she's using it to create more space for what matters most.

This approach to life systems is something every working mother can adopt, regardless of your industry. The goal isn't to optimize every moment of your life, but to create reliable structures that handle the routine decisions so you can focus your mental energy on the bigger picture.

Owning Your Power Without Permission

The most powerful moment in our conversation came when Hannah talked about learning to own her power without waiting for external validation or permission. At 26, she's already grasped something that takes many women decades to understand: your power isn't something someone else gives you - it's something you claim.

This is particularly important for young women who might feel like they need to prove themselves before they can step into leadership roles. Hannah is demonstrating that leadership isn't about age or experience level - it's about taking responsibility for your choices and their outcomes.

She's not waiting until her kids are older, or until she has more experience, or until her life looks more traditional. She's building her career and claiming her space in the tech industry right now, as a single mother in her twenties, because she understands that there's never going to be a perfect time.

This mindset shift is everything. When you stop waiting for permission to pursue your goals, you start creating opportunities that wouldn't have existed otherwise.

The Integration Challenge

One of the ongoing themes in Hannah's story is the challenge of integrating motherhood with professional ambition. She's not trying to compartmentalize these parts of her identity - she's finding ways to let them strengthen each other.

Her children see her working hard and pursuing goals, which teaches them that women can be both nurturing mothers and ambitious professionals. Her experience as a mother brings perspective to her work that her colleagues value. Rather than seeing these roles as competing priorities, she's finding ways to let them complement each other.

This integration isn't always smooth or easy, but it's authentic to who she is and what her life requires right now. She's not trying to be the perfect mother or the perfect employee - she's trying to be fully present in whatever role requires her attention in the moment.

Hannah's story is a reminder that you don't have to choose between being a good mother and having professional ambitions. You can honor both parts of yourself, even when it's messy and imperfect and nothing like what you originally planned.

If you're navigating similar waters - whether you're a young mother, changing careers, or just trying to figure out how to own your power in your current season - Hannah's story offers both inspiration and practical wisdom. The path forward isn't about waiting for perfect conditions. It's about taking the next right step from exactly where you are today.
